title: Evaluation of the Patient With Hypophosphatemia

items.title: Why serum phosphate is often overlooked in musculoskeletal complaints
items.text: Chronic hypophosphatemia causes rickets or osteomalacia; serum phosphorus is frequently omitted from the evaluation of musculoskeletal symptoms, delaying diagnosis for years.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Serum phosphorus is an important mineral that is frequently overlooked in the evaluation of patients with musculoskeletal complaints, which can lead to significant delays in diagnosis and treatment."

items.title: How age-appropriate phosphate reference ranges prevent missed diagnosis
items.text: Infants and children have significantly higher serum phosphate than adults; applying adult reference intervals to children can mask hypophosphatemia.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Serum phosphate concentrations are significantly higher in infants and younger children than in adults. As a result, hypophosphatemia may be overlooked if the adult reference range is applied to the pediatric population."

items.title: How to categorize hypophosphatemia for evaluation
items.text: Three categories guide workup: decreased intake/malabsorption, redistribution, and renal phosphate wasting (FGF-23-mediated vs non-FGF-23-mediated).
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Hypophosphatemia can be divided into 3 main categories (Table): 1. Decreased intake and/or malabsorption 2. Redistribution within the body 3. Renal phosphate wasting"

items.title: How low fractional excretion of phosphate identifies intake/malabsorption
items.text: Low urine phosphate with fractional excretion of phosphate <5% is characteristic of inadequate intake or malabsorption; elevated 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D is also expected.
items.reference: Laboratory Assessment: "Low urine phosphate with a low fractional excretion of phosphate (less than 5%) is a hallmark of hypophosphatemia caused by inadequate intake or malabsorption."

items.title: Why acute respiratory alkalosis causes transient hypophosphatemia
items.text: Acute alkalosis shifts phosphate intracellularly; it is self-limited and does not require phosphate replacement once the underlying cause resolves.
items.reference: Redistribution: "Acute respiratory alkalosis can cause transient hypophosphatemia due to transcellular shifts, is self-limited, and does not require phosphate replacement therapy."

items.title: How FGF-23-mediated renal wasting differs biochemically
items.text: FGF-23-mediated disease shows normal calcium, normal or low urinary calcium, and low or inappropriately normal 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D.
items.reference: Laboratory Assessment: "FGF-23 mediated: Normal serum calcium Normal or low urinary calcium Low or inappropriately normal 1α,25(OH)2D3"

items.title: How burosumab changes XLH management
items.text: Anti-FGF-23 antibody (burosumab) reduces renal phosphate wasting, raises 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, and is given every 2 weeks in children and every 4 weeks in adults.
items.reference: FGF-23-Mediated Disease: "Burosumab treatment results in reduced renal phosphate wasting and increased 1α,25(OH)2D3 concentrations."

items.title: Why calcitriol plus phosphate requires vigilant monitoring
items.text: Conventional XLH therapy risks secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism, hypercalciuria, nephrocalcinosis, and nephrolithiasis—tertiary hyperparathyroidism may require parathyroid surgery.
items.reference: FGF-23-Mediated Disease: "Treatment with calcitriol and phosphate supplementation requires vigilant monitoring to avoid complications such as secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism, hypercalciuria, nephrocalcinosis, and nephrolithiasis."

items.title: How to manage tumor-induced osteomalacia
items.text: Optimal treatment is tumor localization and surgical removal; when elusive or unresectable, calcitriol and phosphate are required—burosumab is not approved for TIO.
items.reference: FGF-23-Mediated Disease: "Optimal treatment for patients with tumor-induced osteomalacia includes identification of the offending tumor and surgical removal."

items.title: Why periodic phosphate monitoring is mandatory after TIO surgery
items.text: Recurrent hypophosphatemia can occur years after apparent cure; phosphate often falls before symptoms or osteomalacia recur.
items.reference: Case 2 (Continued): "Patients with tumor-induced osteomalacia should be monitored proactively after apparent surgical cure. The best recommendation is periodic serum phosphate measurement (Answer B)."

items.title: How hungry bone syndrome differs from TIO postoperative course
items.text: Hungry bone follows parathyroidectomy; serum phosphate typically normalizes within days after TIO tumor resection, though pain and fractures may take months to heal.
items.reference: Case 4 (Continued): "Hungry bone syndrome may occur after parathyroidectomy for hyperparathyroidism; however, serum phosphate typically returns to normal within days after surgical cure of tumor-induced osteomalacia"

items.title: Why phosphate replacement does not improve outcomes in DKA
items.text: Despite sometimes severe hypophosphatemia during DKA treatment, phosphate replacement has not been shown to improve outcomes.
items.reference: Treatment: "Although hypophosphatemia occurring treatment of DKA can be severe, it is not replacement improves outcomes."

items.question: A 21-year-old woman with XLH on calcitriol and phosphorus presents with calcium 13.2 mg/dL and PTH 244 pg/mL one month after stopping supplements. Best initial management?
items.explanation: Tertiary hyperparathyroidism from overzealous phosphate therapy requires parathyroid surgery before resuming XLH treatment.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: D) Consult a parathyroid surgeon"

items.question: The same XLH patient restarts burosumab after pseudofractures and dental abscesses. What is a reasonable treatment expectation?
options: Healing of pseudofractures and reduced osteomalacia-related bone pain
items.explanation: Burosumab heals pseudofractures and reduces osteomalacia bone pain; it does not treat osteoarthritis, enthesopathy, or dental abscess frequency.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "Answer: A) Reduced bone pain and healing of her pseudofractures"

items.question: A 40-year-old man has fractures, low phosphate (1.8 mg/dL), elevated ALP, and a firm neck lesion. Best next step?
options: Refer to medical genetics for heritable hypophosphatemia
Obtain urine studies to assess tubular reabsorption of phosphorus
items.explanation: Osteomalacia with hypophosphatemia requires urinary phosphate assessment to identify renal wasting etiology.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: C) Obtain urine studies to assess tubular reabsorption of phosphorus"

items.question: Fractional excretion of phosphorus is elevated. Which lab pattern is most consistent with elevated FGF-23?
items.explanation: FGF-23 inhibits 1α-hydroxylase, producing low or inappropriately normal 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D.
items.reference: Case 2 (Continued): "Answer: C) Low 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D"

items.question: TIO is suspected and a 2.5×3-cm firm lesion is at the neck base. Best next step?
items.explanation: Cutaneous phosphaturic mesenchymal tumors occur; biopsy of the accessible neck lesion is diagnostic and therapeutic.
items.reference: Case 2 (Continued): "Answer: D) Perform biopsy of skin lesion at the base of his neck"

items.question: After curative TIO resection, best long-term follow-up?
options: Annual whole-body PET regardless of symptoms
No follow-up needed after surgical cure
items.explanation: Proactive phosphate monitoring detects recurrence before symptomatic osteomalacia returns.
items.reference: Case 2 (Continued): "Answer: B) Periodic serum phosphate measurement"

items.question: A 27-year-old woman has acute dyspnea, tachypnea, phosphate 1.4 mg/dL, and anxiety. Best next evaluation step?
options: Obtain arterial or venous blood gas
items.explanation: Acute anxiety with tachypnea suggests respiratory alkalosis causing transcellular hypophosphatemia—confirm with blood gas.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: C) Obtain arterial or venous gas"

items.question: Venous blood gas confirms respiratory alkalosis from acute anxiety. Best management?
options: Vitamin D 50,000 IU weekly for 6 weeks
items.explanation: Treating anxiety reverses alkalosis and restores phosphate rapidly; IV phosphate is unnecessary for transcellular shift.
items.reference: Case 3 (Continued): "Answer: A) Administer a benzodiazepine"

items.question: After treatment, phosphate normalizes. Which features were inconsistent with chronic hypophosphatemia?
items.explanation: Acute onset, normal ALP, lack of bone pain/fractures, and normal childhood growth all favor acute rather than chronic hypophosphatemia.
items.reference: Case 3 (Continued): "Answer: E) All of the above"

items.question: A 70-year-old man has 7 years of TIO symptoms, elevated FGF-23, FEPhos 38%, and unresectable tumor. Best treatment?
options: Physical therapy and pain clinic only
Calcitriol and phosphate or anti-FGF-23 antibody therapy
items.explanation: Unresectable TIO requires medical therapy with calcitriol/phosphate; osteomalacia is not osteoporosis and antiresorptives/anabolics are inappropriate.
items.reference: Case 4: "Answer: B) Initiate medical therapy with calcitriol and phosphate or anti-FGF-23 antibody therapy"

items.question: Six months of medical therapy leaves unhealed fractures. Best next step?
options: Additional functional whole-body imaging (e.g., 68Ga DOTATATE PET)
Arterial blood gas for respiratory alkalosis
items.explanation: After TIO diagnosis, whole-body functional imaging should be pursued even if prior scans were negative.
items.reference: Case 4 (Continued): "Answer: B) Obtain functional whole-body imaging (eg, 68Ga DOTATATE PET)"

items.question: After complete TIO tumor resection, which outcome is expected?
options: Fractures and bone pain may take several months to resolve
Serum phosphate stays low for weeks due to hungry bone syndrome
Medical therapy required for weeks because of long FGF-23 half-life
items.explanation: Surgical cure is achieved but prolonged osteomalacia means pain and fractures may resolve over weeks to months.
items.reference: Case 4 (Continued): "Answer: A) Fractures and bone pain may take several months to resolve after surgical cure"

items.question: A patient with renal Fanconi syndrome and hypophosphatemia requires phosphate supplementation. Is calcitriol indicated?
options: Yes — always combine calcitriol with phosphate
items.explanation: Non-FGF-23 renal wasting without calcitriol deficiency should not receive calcitriol—it worsens hypercalciuria and stone risk.
items.reference: Other: "Because these are not FGF-23-mediated conditions, calcitriol is not indicated as it would worsen the hypercalciuria and lead to nephrocalcinosis and nephrolithiasis."

items.statement: Severe acute hypophosphatemia can cause rhabdomyolysis and hemolysis.
items.explanation: Cardiac, respiratory, rhabdomyolysis, and hemolysis may occur with severe acute hypophosphatemia.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Cardiac and respiratory dysfunction, as well as rhabdomyolysis and hemolysis, may occur with severe, acute hypophosphatemia."

items.statement: All adults with XLH require ongoing pharmacologic therapy after linear growth is complete.
items.explanation: Not all adults with XLH are symptomatic; therapy is individualized after growth completion.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "Not all adults with XLH are symptomatic and therefore not all require pharmacologic intervention."

items.statement: Acute respiratory alkalosis requires phosphate replacement to prevent complications.
items.explanation: Transcellular hypophosphatemia from acute alkalosis is self-limited and does not require replacement.
items.reference: Redistribution: "Acute respiratory alkalosis can cause transient hypophosphatemia due to transcellular shifts, is self-limited, and does not require phosphate replacement therapy."

items.statement: Burosumab is approved for treatment of tumor-induced osteomalacia.
items.explanation: Anti-FGF-23 antibody is approved for XLH but not other FGF-23-mediated conditions such as TIO.
items.reference: FGF-23-Mediated Disease: "Burosumab has not been approved for treatment of other FGF-23-mediated conditions."

items.statement: Calcium-based phosphate binders can cause hypophosphatemia.
items.explanation: Phosphate binders reduce intestinal phosphate availability and can cause hypophosphatemia.
items.reference: Decreased Intake and/or Malabsorption: "patients receiving phosphate binders such as calcium, sevelamer, or lanthanum carbonate can develop hypophosphatemia due to reduced intestinal phosphate availability."

items.statement: After surgical cure of TIO, no further monitoring is needed.
items.explanation: Recurrent hypophosphatemia can occur years later, including with malignant transformation.
items.reference: Case 2 (Continued): "Several years after his initial surgery, this patient developed recurrence of hypophosphatemia with sarcomatous transformation and metastases."

items.statement: Larger parathyroid adenoma volume and higher alkaline phosphatase increase hungry bone risk after parathyroidectomy.
items.explanation: Adenoma volume, elevated ALP, and older age are risk factors for hungry bone syndrome.
items.reference: Redistribution: "larger parathyroid adenoma volume, higher alkaline phosphatase, and older age are risk factors for this condition."

items.statement: Low BMD in osteomalacia should be treated with bisphosphonates like osteoporosis.
items.explanation: Osteomalacia requires correcting phosphate/vitamin D deficiency; bisphosphonates may worsen disease.
items.reference: Case 4: "He has osteomalacia and therefore a bisphosphonate (Answer C) is not appropriate and may worsen his disease."

items.assertion: Assertion: Restarting phosphorus before treating hyperparathyroidism in XLH will lower serum calcium.
items.reason: Reason: Hyperparathyroidism worsens renal phosphate wasting.
items.explanation: Both false—phosphorus restarts worsen hyperparathyroidism; hyperparathyroidism does worsen phosphaturia but does not make A true.
items.reference: Case 1: "Restarting phosphorus supplementation before addressing her hyperparathyroidism (Answer A) would only exacerbate the hyperparathyroidism."

items.assertion: Assertion: FGF-23-mediated hypophosphatemia is associated with low 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D.
items.explanation: Both true and causally linked—FGF-23 suppresses active vitamin D synthesis.
items.reference: Case 2 (Continued): "FGF-23 not only reduces renal phosphate reabsorption but also inhibits 1α-hydroxylase activity."

items.assertion: Assertion: A benzodiazepine can correct hypophosphatemia in acute respiratory alkalosis.
items.reason: Reason: Treating anxiety reverses alkalosis and transcellular phosphate shift.
items.explanation: Both true—resolving alkalosis restores serum phosphate without phosphate supplementation.
items.reference: Case 3 (Continued): "Treatment of this patient's anxiety (Answer A) will reverse her acute respiratory alkalosis and quickly restore serum phosphate to a normal range."

items.assertion: Assertion: Serum phosphate normalizes within days after complete TIO tumor resection.
items.reason: Reason: FGF-23 has a short half-life.
items.explanation: Both true—rapid FGF-23 clearance restores phosphate quickly, unlike hungry bone after parathyroidectomy.
items.reference: Case 4 (Continued): "FGF-23 has a short, not long, half-life, and medical therapy after complete resection is not required"

items.assertion: Assertion: Phosphate should be routinely replaced during DKA treatment to improve outcomes.
items.reason: Reason: DKA treatment commonly causes severe hypophosphatemia.
items.explanation: R is true but A is false—severe hypophosphatemia occurs yet replacement does not improve outcomes.
items.reference: Treatment: "Although hypophosphatemia occurring treatment of DKA can be severe, it is not replacement improves outcomes."

items.assertion: Assertion: Burosumab reduces the frequency of dental abscess in XLH.
items.reason: Reason: Burosumab improves healing of pseudofractures.
items.explanation: Both false—burosumab may increase dental abscess vs conventional therapy; pseudofracture healing is a separate outcome.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "there is no evidence that anti-FGF-23 antibody therapy improves pain associated with osteoarthritis (Answer B) or enthesopathy (Answer C) or reduces the frequency of dental abscess (Answer D)."

items.assertion: Assertion: Fractional excretion of phosphate <5% suggests inadequate intake or malabsorption.
items.reason: Reason: Renal phosphate wasting elevates fractional excretion.
items.explanation: Both true—low FEPhos indicates non-renal cause; the reason correctly contrasts with renal wasting.
items.reference: Laboratory Assessment: "Low urine phosphate with a low fractional excretion of phosphate (less than 5%) is a hallmark of hypophosphatemia caused by inadequate intake or malabsorption."

items.assertion: Assertion: Alendronate is appropriate first-line therapy for TIO with fractures.
items.reason: Reason: The patient has low bone density on DXA.
items.explanation: Both false—osteomalacia not osteoporosis; low BMD reflects defective mineralization, not an indication for bisphosphonates.
items.reference: Case 2: "His low bone density is related to osteomalacia and not osteoporosis."
